risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The clusters of occupational physicians were randomized by an independent statistician using a computer algorithm for randomization... At GGz Breburg, only 1 occupational physician was available. For this reason, a cluster crossover design was used at first with the first 100 employees approached as the control condition and subsequently the second 100 employees approached as the intervention condition... Therefore, this can be considered as a pseudo-randomization design in GGz Breburg." Comment: Although most clusters at Arbo Vitale used a proper random method (independent statistician, computer algorithm), the paper explicitly states that allocation at GGz Breburg was based on a non-random, predictable rule (first 100 control, second 100 intervention) and describes it as pseudo-randomization. This is positive evidence of non-random sequence generation in part of the study, so the domain is High risk.

Quote: "At GGz Breburg, only 1 occupational physician was available. For this reason, a cluster crossover design was used at first with the first 100 employees approached as the control condition and subsequently the second 100 employees approached as the intervention condition... Therefore, this can be considered as a pseudo-randomization design in GGz Breburg." Comment: This allocation procedure was predictable by order of approach, so the upcoming assignment could be foreseen before enrolment at that site. Although the paper also states, "The research assistants and the participants were blind to the allocation when assessing the eligibility of sick-listed employees," the explicit pseudo-randomized first-100/second-100 procedure provides positive evidence that allocation was foreseeable for at least part of the trial.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Because the occupational physicians had to guide the intervention, they could not be blinded to the group assignment after randomization... After the questionnaire was filled out and informed consent was given, the participant was informed by the researchers via telephone about the allocation." Quote: "Participants completed online self-report questionnaires at baseline (T0) and at 3 (T1), 6 (T2), 9 (T3), and 12 months (T4) after inclusion." Comment: Both personnel delivering the intervention and participants were unblinded after enrolment/allocation. The study included outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, especially self-reported symptom outcomes (depression, anxiety, somatization/remission/response) and process outcomes. Therefore performance bias is at high risk overall, despite RTW register-based outcomes being more objective.

Quote: "After the questionnaire was filled out and informed consent was given, the participant was informed by the researchers via telephone about the allocation." and "Participants completed online self-report questionnaires at baseline (T0) and at 3 (T1), 6 (T2), 9 (T3), and 12 months (T4) after inclusion." and "Secondary outcome measures were the severity of depression, anxiety, and somatization symptoms as measured with the PHQ-9, GAD-7, and PHQ-15 in terms of response and remission." Comment: Outcome assessment included patient self-report for secondary outcomes, so the patient was the outcome assessor. Participants were explicitly unblinded before follow-up assessment, and these outcomes are subjective symptom questionnaires that could be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Although RTW outcomes came from registers and are relatively objective, the study also included unblinded subjective self-reported outcomes; by the worst-outcome rule, the overall detection bias judgement is High risk.

Quote: "Data about RTW were obtained from the registers of the OHS or employer. Sickness absence data were available for 86 employees in the control condition and for 130 employees in the intervention condition. For unknown reasons, the sickness absence data of 4 participants could not be found in the registers." and "For the self-reported secondary outcomes, follow-up questionnaires were returned by 158 of 220 participants (71.8%) at 3 months, 158 participants (71.8%) at 6 months, 137 participants (62.3%) at 9 months, and 131 participants (59.5%) at 12 months. At 9 months, the loss to follow-up rate was significantly higher in the ECO condition (44.3%, 58/131) than in the CAU condition (28%, 25/89, P=.02)." Comment: Attrition was minimal for the register-based RTW outcome (3/89 CAU and 1/131 ECO missing), but for self-reported outcomes there was substantial overall missing data by 12 months (89/220 missing, 40.5%) and clear differential attrition at 9 months (44.3% vs 28.0%; difference 16.3 percentage points). These reported numbers cross Cochrane thresholds for high attrition bias for the self-reported outcomes of interest, and no robust missing-data strategy is reported that would remove this concern.
